role_hints.oews_basis: 27-4021 Photographers 50.7% of wage bill (5-digit 54192 mix) — an unusually concentrated single-occupation industry
role_hints.note: The in-person capture (posing a family, running a school photo day, shooting a wedding) is physical and not automatable; but half or more of a portrait photographer's hours — culling, editing, retouching, album layout — is already substantially AI-automated (Imagen, Aftershoot class tools). Separately, AI image generation attacks the product itself (headshots from selfies); keep that in terminal value, not in the cost-side fraction.
Event videography is physical; editing and highlight-reel production is heavily AI-automatable.
Scheduling, studio ops reporting and marketing automation are AI-tractable; school-district and league contract relationships persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.0% + 41-2031 Retail Salespersons 1.9% + 43-4051 Customer Service Reps 1.9%
role_hints.note: Online proofing galleries and automated upsell flows replace in-person sales sessions; high-touch IPS (in-person sales) studios keep humans because the sales session IS the margin model.

role_hints.oews_basis: 51-9151 Photographic Process Workers and Processing Machine Operators 3.1%
role_hints.note: Lab/print production and order fulfillment automate near-fully; most studios already outsource to consolidated labs.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-9061 Office Clerks 4.3% + 43-3031 Bookkeeping Clerks 2.1% + unlisted admin tail est. ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Booking, order entry, invoicing, gallery delivery and customer comms automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: PPA (Professional Photographers of America) benchmark surveys and member research
source_hints.why: The main US trade association; publishes studio financial benchmarks and has surveyed members on AI tools and AI-generation threat.

source_hints.source: Rangefinder / WPPI (Wedding & Portrait Photographers International) industry coverage
source_hints.why: Trade coverage of AI editing-tool adoption (Aftershoot, Imagen) in wedding/portrait workflows — concrete adoption evidence.

source_hints.source: Shutterfly/Lifetouch corporate disclosures and school-photography contract coverage
source_hints.why: Lifetouch (acquired by Shutterfly 2018) dominates contracted school/volume portraiture — the one consolidated, contract-locked sub-segment; its pricing model is the capture benchmark.
Small studios trade as SDE-multiple main-street deals; BizBuySell is the realistic comp source — there is no institutional multiple universe for this code.
Owner-age and studio-closure data if published; the industry is 90% micro-firms so succession is mostly closure, not sale — verify what PPA actually reports.

capture_questions: Portrait work is priced as session fees plus print/package upsells (or per-child package prices in contracted school photography) — when AI cuts editing and admin hours, does the solo-studio market's low entry barrier and price competition pass the savings to consumers immediately, or can branded studios hold session prices and pocket the margin?

Who owns the client relationship — in volume photography it is the school district/league contract (institutional, renewable, owned by Lifetouch-class players); in retail portraiture it is personal to the photographer whose style the family chose. Which book is a roll-up actually buying, and does retail revenue survive the founder leaving?

What stops a consumer from using a $20 AI headshot app or generating family portraits from phone photos instead of paying $200-500 for a session — and for which occasions (newborn, school, sports, weddings) does the physical event itself keep demand alive?

In contracted school/volume photography, does the district RFP process claw AI savings back as lower per-package prices or higher commissions to the school, rather than leaving margin with the operator?
terminal_value_question: This is a melter candidate: AI image generation attacks the product, not just the costs — a likeness no longer requires a photographer. The mechanism that decides survival is the physical occasion: school photo day, youth sports day, weddings and newborn sessions are events where presence, herding kids and the ritual itself are the product, while 'I need a headshot/portrait' demand is already migrating to generative apps. Score the terminal question segment by segment: how much of the code's revenue is occasion-locked physical events vs. output-only portraiture that generative AI replaces outright — and would a researcher notice that strong current session demand says nothing about the 5-year output-only segment?
special_notes: Melter + no targets: n_band is 21 firms — effectively zero roll-up universe. 8,742 of 9,682 firms are <5 employees with avg revenue ~$200K; the only firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band are the 100-499 class (21 firms). Any roll-up thesis here is buying micro-SDE businesses or competing with Shutterfly/Lifetouch in contracted volume work; score N and the overall opportunity accordingly.

EBITDA normalization: studio 'profit' is almost always SDE with the owner-photographer's labor inside; market-rate replacement cost for the shooter typically erases most of the margin. Multiples are main-street SDE multiples (often <3x), not EBITDA multiples — do not import professional-services comps.

Demand structure: the record's IPS-based labor share (0.2051, HIGH quality) is strikingly low for a personal-services code — consistent with heavy pass-through (prints, labs) and owner-proprietor labor not appearing in wages. Nonemployer firms vastly outnumber the 9,682 employer firms; SUSB-based counts understate the competitive fringe that sets prices.

Boundary confusion: commercial photography is 541922 (separate code, different client base), photofinishing labs are 812921/812922, school-picture composite printing sits with the lab, and freelance photographers without employees are nonemployers outside SUSB — PPA membership and industry commentary span all of them; check segment before quoting.

Sub-segment mix drives everything: contracted volume (school/sports/graduation — consolidated, contract-locked, Lifetouch-dominated), weddings/events (occasion-locked, fragmented), retail portrait studios (declining since the JCPenney/Sears studio collapse), and headshot/personal-branding work (most exposed to generative AI). State the mix explicitly before scoring V or terminal value.

Golden-set note: this code anchors the melter end of the instrument — be conservative about anything that looks like a cost-savings windfall; the dominant effect is product substitution and it belongs in terminal value / pi_dist, not in a rosy automation margin.
